content.verdict: Pink Sands Resort is set on the bluff above its namesake — Pink Sands Beach on Harbour Island, considered by many travel publications to be the most beautiful beach in the world. The 20-acre property is owned by music producer Chris Blackwell (Island Records) and has the unhurried, hip-bohemian character that defines his portfolio. The 25 cottages, the Garden Terrace dining, and the direct access to three miles of pink sand make it Harbour Island's most iconic address.
content.best_room: The Beachfront Cottages are the property's flagship — standalone bungalows with private terraces facing the pink sand. The Tropical Garden Cottages sit deeper in the property's lush grounds, slightly less expensive and equally charming. The Diamond Cottages are the most spacious. Honeymooners should request a Beachfront Cottage on the dune line — the sound of the surf and direct beach access make it singularly romantic.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Arrival on Harbour Island
itinerary_7_nights.description: Fly to North Eleuthera, then a 5-minute taxi to the Three Island Dock and a 10-minute water taxi crossing to Harbour Island — one of the world's great small-island arrivals. Golf carts are the only transport. Settle into your beachfront cottage, walk the pink sand at sunset, and have the first dinner at the Garden Terrace with citronella torches lit through the trees.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Pink Sands Beach Day
itinerary_7_nights.description: Pink Sands Beach gets its color from microscopic foraminifera shells crushed into the sand — most pronounced at sunrise and sunset. Spend the day on the property's stretch with chairs and umbrellas set up by the staff. Lunch at the Beach Bar, afternoon swim, sunset stroll the full three-mile beach. Cocktails at the Blue Bar before dinner.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Dunmore Town & Loyalist Cottages
itinerary_7_nights.description: Spend the morning exploring Dunmore Town — the only settlement on Harbour Island, one of the oldest in the Bahamas. The pastel-painted Loyalist cottages, the white picket fences, the bougainvillea-draped lanes are golf-cart explorable in an hour. Stop at Sip Sip on the bluff for the famous conch chowder and a piña colada. Afternoon back on the beach.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Snorkeling & Devil's Backbone
itinerary_7_nights.description: The Devil's Backbone reef just off Harbour Island has historically claimed many ships — today it's a snorkeling and shipwreck-diving site with dramatic visibility. A half-day snorkeling charter visits multiple sites including the Cienfuegos and Carnarvon wrecks. Afternoon back at Pink Sands' pool with rum cocktails.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Spa Day & Golf Cart Adventure
itinerary_7_nights.description: Morning couples massage at the resort spa, then rent a golf cart for the afternoon to explore Harbour Island's quieter eastern side — Girl's Bank, the local cemetery, and the lookout points over the Atlantic. Dinner at Rock House on the bluff (a sister property, a 5-minute cart ride away) for elevated Bahamian cuisine.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Eleuthera Day Trip
itinerary_7_nights.description: Take the water taxi back across to Eleuthera and rent a car for the day. Drive south to the Glass Window Bridge for the dramatic Atlantic-Caribbean color contrast, then continue to Preacher's Cave and the Queen's Bath rock pools. Return to Harbour Island by late afternoon for sunset on Pink Sands Beach.

itinerary_7_nights.title: Final Pink Sand Sunrise & Departure
itinerary_7_nights.description: Wake early for a sunrise walk on Pink Sands Beach — the color is at its most intense at dawn. Final breakfast at the Garden Terrace, then the water taxi back to North Eleuthera for the connection home through Nassau.

caveats: Pink Sands Resort is family-friendly but the Beachfront Cottages skew honeymoon-heavy; in school holidays the wider property and beach can be busier with families.
Harbour Island is small and getting there requires a connection (Nassau or Florida to North Eleuthera, then water taxi) — total travel time from US East Coast cities is 6 to 8 hours.
The pink color of Pink Sands Beach is real but subtle; it's most visible at sunrise, sunset, and where waves wet the sand. Visitors expecting hot pink can be initially underwhelmed.

faqs.question: Good for honeymooners?
faqs.answer: Pink Sands Resort is one of the Bahamas' most romantic addresses — the Chris Blackwell aesthetic, the iconic beach, the standalone cottages, and the unhurried Harbour Island culture make it ideal for couples wanting hip-bohemian luxury rather than corporate resort polish. The Beachfront Cottages on the dune line are exceptionally romantic.

faqs.question: Best time to visit?
faqs.answer: November through May is the dry season. December through April is peak — book early. The pink sand looks remarkable year-round but is most striking with strong morning or evening light. Hurricane season (August-October) is best avoided despite reduced rates.

faqs.question: Book in advance?
faqs.answer: Pink Sands sells out 4 to 6 months ahead for high season. Beachfront Cottages are the first to go. Christmas, New Year, and February book up nearly a year in advance for couples who want the dune-line cottages.

faqs.question: Adults-only?
faqs.answer: Pink Sands is not adults-only; families do stay particularly during school holidays. Mid-week and shoulder season have a markedly more adult/couple-heavy atmosphere. The cottage layout means even when families are on property, couples can find privacy.

faqs.question: Best room type?
faqs.answer: Beachfront Cottages on the dune line are the most romantic — direct beach access and the sound of the surf at night. Tropical Garden Cottages are more shaded and equally charming at a lower price point. Diamond Cottages offer the most space.

faqs.question: How to get there?
faqs.answer: North Eleuthera airport is the gateway — 30-minute flights from Nassau or seasonal direct flights from Florida. From the airport, a 5-minute taxi to the Three Island Dock and a 10-minute water taxi reach Harbour Island. Pink Sands arranges transfers.
